text: The video shows a right indirect inguinal hernia, with visible protrusion of abdominal contents through the inguinal canal.
The surgeon begins dissection at the medial umbilical ligament, proceeds down to the supravesicular space, crosses into the preperitoneal plane, and extends laterally towards Bogros' space.
The preperitoneal plane is clearly visible, showing a well-defined separation of tissue layers.
The surgeon observes the separation of tissue planes, noting that they are not fused together and separate cleanly.
The surgeon carefully maintains a thin lateral flap while pushing the surrounding fat laterally to protect the nerves in the surgical field.

The surgeon works on the anterior portion, focusing on the preperitoneal plane that has been established, extending the dissection laterally from the medial umbilical ligament towards the supravesicular space.
The surgeon carefully maintains dissection along the sac, ensuring precise separation within the established preperitoneal plane.
The surgeon dissects down to Cooper's ligament and the pubic tubercle, extending the dissection medially beyond the symphysis pubis.
The surgeon uses a cautery device with gentle retraction inferiorly, avoiding excessive instrument spreading to minimize bleeding and prevent tearing of the corona mortis.
The surgeon switches to an oblique angle and uses electrocautery to buzz directly on the hernia sac, while pushing the surrounding fat away from the surgical field.
The surgeon uses electrocautery to buzz directly on top of the hernia sac, occasionally entering the sac to reorient the surgical approach, while working on the anterior portion.
The surgeon approaches the epigastric vessels, which appear to be adhered to surrounding tissue, requiring careful dissection.
The surgeon continues to push the fat down, maintaining clear visibility of the underlying structures.
The surgeon pulls down on the chord lipoma, applying traction to separate it from surrounding tissues.

The surgeon carefully teases the lipoma off the chord structures, maintaining proximity to these structures without extending dissection into the retroperitoneum. The nerve is visible running anterior to the chord structures.
The surgeon cuts away fatty structures surrounding the chord lipoma, carefully separating them from adjacent anatomical structures.
The surgeon maintains tension at the deep internal ring, with visible tautness in the surrounding tissue.
The vas deferens and spermatic cord vessels come into view as the surgeon manipulates surrounding tissue.
The surgeon observes a small amount of fatty tissue on the spermatic cord, noting that its appearance is within normal limits.
The iliac artery is visibly pulsating in the background as the surgeon carefully stays medial to the vas deferens, ensuring a safe dissection path.

The surgeon identifies and separates creeping fat from the femoral space, finding a natural plane and taking it down to the obturator fossa. The obturator plug is typically removed during this process.
The surgeon extends the dissection beyond the symphysis pubis, revealing a flap while ensuring that the surrounding fat is kept laterally.
The surgeon performs a typical flap closure using a V-lock suture, securing the flap in place.
